title: Nom Nom Dog Food Review 2026: Personalized Portions, Real Ingredients Tested
description: Tested Nom Nom fresh dog food for 60 days on a 47-lb Lab mix: palatability, pricing, ingredient quality, and whether the portion algorithm actually works.
verdict: Nom Nom delivers genuinely high-quality fresh food with a legitimate nutrition team and well-calibrated portions, but freezer logistics and packaging frustrations limit long-term practicality for many households.

amazon.product: Outward Hound Fun Feeder Slow Bowl

content:
I've been feeding Bruno — my 47-pound, neutered, four-year-old Lab mix — fresh food on and off for the past two years. He came to me from a rescue with a coat that looked like weathered straw and stools that oscillated between pudding and cement. Getting his digestion sorted took six months and three different food brands. So when I say I approached this Nom Nom review with genuine curiosity rather than pre-formed skepticism, I mean it.

What I wanted to know was simple: does Nom Nom's personalization actually mean anything nutritionally, or is it a subscription wrapper around a standard fresh-food formula? Does the portion algorithm account for Bruno being neutered — a detail that matters because neutered males typically need 25–30% fewer calories than intact dogs at the same weight and activity level? And does the quality hold up after 60 days of real use, not just the first glamour delivery?

I tested all six Nom Nom recipes over 60 days. Here is what I actually found.

---

## Quick Verdict

**Top Pick:** Nom Nom Chicken Cuisine — highest protein (37% as-fed), best stool quality in my testing, Bruno's sustained favorite after the initial novelty wore off.

**Palatability Pick:** Nom Nom Beef Mash — fastest bowl clearance, most consistent acceptance across the dogs in my extended testing group. As one owner in A Couple Consumers noted, "The Beef Mash was his favorite. He ate it right away and licked the bowl clean."

**Budget Alternative:** [The Farmer's Dog](https://petverdict.net/go/farmersdog) — roughly $95/month less expensive for a 30-lb dog (~$4.57/day vs Nom Nom's ~$5.80–6.00/day). Ingredient quality is comparable. If cost is the deciding factor, Farmer's Dog is the honest alternative.

---

## Testing Methodology

I ran a 60-day protocol with Bruno as the primary test subject, with palatability observations from a four-dog extended group (22–68 lbs) recruited through a local training co-op. I photographed each delivery, tested all six recipes in rotation, and tracked stool quality daily using a 1–7 scale (3–4 is ideal). Coat texture, energy level, and digestive consistency served as owner-observable health proxies. Bloodwork was not conducted — that requires a controlled clinical design. Pricing was verified on the Nom Nom website in April 2026.

---

## First Impressions: Unboxing and Packaging

The first delivery arrived in an insulated box with dry ice — standard for fresh subscription food. The presentation inside is clean: labeled individual meal packs, a free storage tub included with subscription (a genuine value-add as of 2026), and a printed feeding guide specific to Bruno's profile.

Then I got to the rubber bands.

The meal packs are bundled together with rubber bands — grouped presumably for logistical efficiency. The problem is structural: the bands are applied at room temperature, and as the packs freeze and expand, the rubber cuts into the packaging material at the contact points. In my first delivery, two packs had visible indentations from the bands. In my third delivery, one pack had a hairline seal compromise at a band groove — not a full rupture, but enough to make me inspect every pack before storage.

This is not a new complaint. A ConsumerAffairs reviewer in 2025 described it precisely: "The food is fine but the packaging is absolutely atrocious — packs come rubber banded together and because they are banded at room temperature, the rubber bands cut into the packaging." I tested across four deliveries. The rubber band issue appeared in three of them.

The free storage tub is a genuine positive. It is substantial, well-sealed, and holds several days of thawed portions cleanly in the fridge. Nom Nom deserves credit for including it — competitors typically charge for accessories or leave you sourcing your own containers.

---

## Ingredient Analysis

### Nom Nom Chicken Cuisine

**Best for: High-protein maintenance, dogs transitioning from kibble needing maximum digestibility improvement**

**Price: ~$5.80–6.00/day for a 47-lb dog (monthly) | First order: 50% off + free shipping**

**First 5 ingredients:** Chicken, chicken liver, russet potatoes, carrots, spinach

Chicken Cuisine leads the Nom Nom lineup at 37% protein on an as-fed basis. That number is not comparable to a kibble label. At roughly 75% moisture, the dry matter basis (DMB) calculation works like this: 37% as-fed ÷ 25% dry matter fraction = approximately 148% protein on a DMB basis. That sounds impossible until you understand that DMB percentages express nutrient concentration relative only to the dry fraction — the honest translation is that Chicken Cuisine is genuinely protein-dense and outperforms most high-protein kibbles on a like-for-like DMB comparison.

Whole chicken — not chicken meal — heads the ingredient list. Whole chicken contains significant water weight and drops in ranking after cooking. Chicken meal (the water-extracted, concentrated form) would rank higher post-cook, but it is unusual in minimally-processed fresh food. This is physics, not manipulation, and the formulation choice is appropriate for the category.

Carbohydrate content is not published directly. Derived from the guaranteed analysis (subtracting protein, fat, moisture, ash, and fiber from 100), Chicken Cuisine yields approximately 18–22% carbohydrates as-fed — moderate and within normal range for a complete-and-balanced fresh diet.

WSAVA context: Nom Nom's AAFCO compliance statement — "All Nom Nom Freshly-made Food recipes meet or exceed the AAFCO Dog Food Nutrition Profile standards for all life stages" — covers the nutrient-profile pathway, not feeding trials. WSAVA recommends manufacturers employ a board-certified nutritionist, publish research, and conduct feeding trials. Nom Nom meets the first two; the feeding-trial gap is the honest WSAVA limitation.

**Pros:**
- Highest protein of all six recipes at 37% as-fed
- Whole chicken and chicken liver as first two ingredients — muscle meat plus organ nutrient density
- Spinach and carrots contribute beta-carotene, lutein, and prebiotic fiber
- Dr. Justin Shmalberg (DACVN) as formulating nutritionist — the highest credential in pet food nutrition
- No FDA or AAFCO recalls for dog food recipes in 2025 or 2026
- AAFCO "all life stages" statement covers puppies through seniors

**Cons:**
- Nutrient-profile AAFCO compliance, not feeding-trial verification
- Whole chicken (water-heavy) overstates ingredient ranking relative to post-cook protein contribution
- Carbohydrate content not published directly — requires manual calculation from guaranteed analysis
- Potato base may not suit dogs with specific carbohydrate sensitivities

---

### Nom Nom Beef Mash

**Best for: Picky eaters, dogs with chicken sensitivity, palatability-first households**

**Price: ~$5.80–6.00/day for a 47-lb dog (monthly) | First order: 50% off + free shipping**

**First 5 ingredients:** Beef, beef liver, russet potatoes, carrots, kale

Beef Mash was the palatability standout across my full extended group — every dog cleared the bowl. The texture is denser than the chicken recipe and the aroma is stronger, which drives the faster acceptance. Beef liver delivers heme iron, zinc, and B12 at concentrations that require significant supplementation to match in a home-prepared diet, and getting organ meat ratios right is precisely where DIY feeding fails without nutritionist oversight.

Kale contains calcium oxalate, which in large quantities can contribute to urinary oxalate crystals in predisposed dogs. In a DACVN-supervised complete-and-balanced formula, this is not a meaningful concern for healthy dogs — but it is relevant context for any dog with a history of calcium oxalate urolithiasis.

Omega-3 transparency is my persistent frustration here. Nom Nom's guaranteed analysis shows omega-6 content but does not independently disclose EPA and DHA. For a fresh food product at this price point, the omega-3 to omega-6 ratio gap is a real transparency issue. If feeding Beef Mash as a sole diet, add a fish oil supplement. Our [7 Dog Fish Oil Supplements Ranked 2026: Vet-Tested Omega-3s](/best-fish-oil-supplements-dogs-2026) covers what to look for.

**Pros:**
- Best palatability in my 60-day test — fastest bowl clearance, most consistent acceptance
- Beef liver provides heme iron, zinc, and B12 at concentrations requiring supplement matching in DIY diets
- Kale adds antioxidants (lutein, vitamin K) alongside the carrot base
- DACVN-formulated, same nutritional oversight as Chicken Cuisine
- No recalls in 2025 or 2026 for dog food recipes

**Cons:**
- Kale contains calcium oxalate — not a concern in healthy dogs but relevant with a history of oxalate urolithiasis
- Omega-3 content (EPA/DHA) not independently disclosed — a transparency gap for a premium-priced product
- Protein slightly lower than Chicken Cuisine on as-fed basis
- Same nutrient-profile AAFCO pathway limitation as all Nom Nom recipes

---

### Nom Nom Pork Potluck

**Best for: Dogs needing protein variety, households rotating proteins to reduce sensitization risk**

**Price: ~$5.80–6.00/day for a 47-lb dog (monthly) | First order: 50% off + free shipping**

**First 5 ingredients:** Pork, pork liver, yellow squash, green beans, butternut squash

Pork Potluck is the protein-lowest recipe at 28% as-fed. The squash and green bean base delivers a lower glycemic profile and more low-calorie fiber bulk than the potato-anchored chicken and beef recipes — useful for dogs needing weight management alongside protein rotation.

Cooked pork is a complete, novel protein for dogs raised on chicken or beef, making this a legitimate option for controlled rotation in allergy-management protocols. The historical misperception about pork and parasites applies to raw or undercooked pork; it is not relevant to commercially cooked fresh food.

Ingredient splitting is worth flagging at this protein level. Some manufacturers list pea protein, pea fiber, and whole peas separately to inflate perceived protein quality cheaply. Nom Nom does not split legume protein sources in Pork Potluck — the ingredients are whole food sources throughout, which is a real quality differentiator.

For dogs prone to eating too fast — Bruno included — I pair Pork Potluck (the thicker texture makes it easier to portion) with an Outward Hound Fun Feeder Slow Bowl ([Check price on Amazon](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B00FPKNRCS?tag=petverdict-20)). Fresh food can be inhaled almost as fast as kibble, and slowing intake down improves digestion and reduces regurgitation risk.

**Pros:**
- Low-calorie vegetable base (squash, green beans) useful for weight-conscious feeding
- Novel protein useful for rotation and allergy-management protocols
- No legume protein splitting — whole food ingredients throughout
- DACVN oversight consistent with full Nom Nom lineup
- Squash base provides meaningful soluble fiber for digestive health

**Cons:**
- Lowest protein of any Nom Nom recipe at 28% as-fed — not appropriate for high-activity or working dogs as sole protein source
- Same omega-3 transparency gap as other recipes
- AAFCO nutrient-profile pathway, not feeding-trial verification
- Pork allergy, while uncommon, does exist — not a valid novel protein for dogs with confirmed pork sensitivity

---

---

## 60-Day Test Results

### Transition (Days 1–14)

I followed Nom Nom's recommended transition protocol: 25% Nom Nom / 75% Bruno's previous food in week one, flipping to 75%/25% in week two, then full Nom Nom by day 14. Bruno tolerated the transition better than expected. By day five, I was seeing the first meaningful stool quality improvement — firmer, lower volume, and more consistent. The low-volume stools matter: they are a primary indicator of digestibility. Less fecal output from the same caloric intake means more of the food was absorbed rather than passed through.

Days 7–10 brought one episode of loose stool that I attribute to too-fast a transition rather than a food quality issue. Slowing the ratio change resolved it within 48 hours.

### Palatability Ranking (All 6 Recipes)

Based on Bruno's bowl-clearance time and enthusiasm, plus observations from the extended group:

1. **Beef Mash** — fastest clearance, most consistent enthusiasm across all dogs tested
2. **Chicken Cuisine** — sustained preference after novelty period (week 3 onward)
3. **Turkey & Chicken Cookout** — strong initial acceptance, slightly slower clearance after week 4
4. **Lamb Pilaf** — above-average, particularly in the two non-Bruno dogs with lamb experience
5. **Turkey Fare** — adequate but clearly lower enthusiasm than beef/chicken
6. **Pork Potluck** — accepted consistently but never the first choice

### Health Observations (Days 15–60)

**Stool quality:** Bruno's stools normalized at a consistent 3–4 on a 1–7 scale by week three and held there for the remainder of the trial. Before Nom Nom, he ranged from 2 to 5 depending on the day.

**Coat:** Noticeable texture improvement by week five — less brittle, more luster. I attribute this partly to the fresh food and partly to the omega-3s I added as a separate supplement (precisely because of Nom Nom's omega-3 transparency gap). I cannot cleanly isolate Nom Nom's contribution to the coat improvement.

**Energy:** No meaningful change. Bruno was already active at the appropriate level for his age and weight. I am skeptical of any reviewer who claims dramatic energy changes from food alone in a healthy adult dog — Bruno's baseline was already appropriate.

**Weight:** Bruno held steady at 47 lbs throughout the trial. This is the most important data point from the portion algorithm section below.

---

## What Surprised Me — Positive

### 1. The Portion Algorithm Actually Accounted for Neutered Status

This was my primary test going in. Most fresh food brands ask for weight, age, and activity level. They do not ask about spay/neuter status. Nom Nom's intake questionnaire explicitly asks. And the portion size Bruno received — verified against a metabolic energy requirement calculator I use professionally — was calibrated appropriately for a neutered male at moderate activity, not a higher intact-dog baseline. Neutered dogs typically need 25–30% fewer calories at the same weight, and Bruno's portions reflected that. Over 60 days of feeding, he did not gain or lose weight. That is the algorithm working correctly.

### 2. The Microbiome Research Is Legitimate

Nom Nom's research program, led by Dr. Ryan Honaker, Director of Microbiology, has published findings in Animal Microbiome — a peer-reviewed journal, not a brand white paper. In a category full of gut-health marketing claims backed by nothing, having externally reviewed research is a genuine differentiator. I remain appropriately skeptical of how that data gets commercialized, but the publication record is real.

### 3. Formula Consistency Across Deliveries

All four deliveries showed consistent color, texture, and ingredient distribution — notably better than some fresh food brands where batch variation is visible. Chicken Cuisine looked and smelled identical in delivery one and delivery four. The Mars Petcare/Royal Canin manufacturing infrastructure likely drives this; standardized production at that scale is not achievable for independent fresh-food startups.

---

## What Frustrated Me — Negative

### 1. The Rubber Band Packaging Problem Remains Unresolved

I contacted Nom Nom customer service after finding packaging damage in my third delivery. The replacement credit was processed without friction — but the underlying issue was not acknowledged, and it recurred. After four deliveries, my workaround is to cut the rubber bands immediately on arrival before packs freeze with the groove embedded. That should not be the customer's job.

### 2. Freezer and Thaw Logistics Add Real Daily Overhead

For a single-dog household with a modest freezer, the logistics of fresh food subscription are not trivial. I keep roughly four days of Nom Nom thawed in the refrigerator at any time, which means managing a weekly thaw cycle, monitoring fridge space, and adjusting for travel or disruption. The A Couple Consumers review team summarized what I observed myself over two months: "Between the cost, freezer space, thawing, serving, and cleanup, it just didn't feel practical long term." This is not a criticism of Nom Nom specifically — it applies to all fresh food subscriptions. But it is a real friction point that many first-time buyers underestimate.

If you use an [auto feeder](/best-automatic-pet-feeders-2026-tested-reliability) or travel frequently, factor in whether the thaw cycle is compatible with your schedule before committing.

### 3. The Omega-3 Transparency Gap

Nom Nom publishes guaranteed analysis for protein, fat, fiber, moisture, and ash. EPA and DHA are not independently disclosed. For a premium fresh food, this matters: whole chicken raised on grain-heavy diets is naturally low in omega-3s, and without a verified fish oil or algae supplement in the formula, the actual EPA/DHA delivered is uncertain. I added Nordic Naturals fish oil as a separate supplement throughout the test — reasonable protocol, but an additional cost that Nom Nom's own formula should be transparent enough to make unnecessary. Our [7 Dog Fish Oil Supplements Ranked 2026: Vet-Tested Omega-3s](/best-fish-oil-supplements-dogs-2026) walks through what to look for.

---

**Cost comparison:** The Farmer's Dog runs ~$4.57/day for a 30-lb dog versus Nom Nom's ~$5.80–6.00/day — roughly $38–50 more per month. The ingredient quality difference is real but not dramatic enough to mandate Nom Nom if cost is a primary constraint. ---

## Use Case Recommendations

### Who Should Buy Nom Nom

- **Single-dog households with adequate freezer space** where the logistics overhead is manageable
- **Dogs with digestive sensitivity** who have not responded well to kibble — the fresh, minimally-processed format genuinely improves stool quality and digestibility for many dogs
- **Owners who want DACVN oversight** and are willing to pay for the credentialing — Dr. Shmalberg's background is legitimate and meaningful
- **Dogs with weight management needs** where the accurate portion algorithm (including spay/neuter adjustment) matters for avoiding slow, unnoticed weight gain
- **Owners interested in protein rotation** — six recipes gives meaningful variety for rotation protocols

### Who Should Skip Nom Nom

- **Multi-dog households** where the cost stacks are significant — the economics become very difficult at two or three dogs
- **Large breed dogs over 80 lbs** where the daily cost approaches $12–15/day — the Farmer's Dog or Spot & Tango offer comparable quality with better large-breed pricing in some weight tiers
- **Frequent travelers** or anyone with a demanding schedule — the thaw cycle and fridge management add daily overhead that disrupts with travel
- **Owners with small freezers** — four-day rotating supply requires meaningful freezer real estate
- **Budget-constrained households** — if the gap between Nom Nom and quality kibble is a financial strain, Purina Pro Plan (which uses feeding trials and has DACVN involvement) is a legitimate complete diet at a fraction of the cost. See our [Best Dog Food 2026: 12 Brands Vet-Tested Over 90 Days](/best-dog-food-2026-vet-tested-and-ranked) for context on how Nom Nom fits into the broader competitive landscape.

---

## Alternatives Worth Considering

### The Farmer's Dog
[The Farmer's Dog](https://petverdict.net/go/farmersdog) — approximately $4.57/day for a 30-lb dog, roughly $95/month less than Nom Nom. Ingredient quality is comparable, DACVN oversight is present, logistics are similar. If price is the barrier, start here.

### Ollie
[Ollie](https://petverdict.net/go/ollie) runs ~$9.75/day — significantly more than Nom Nom. Acquired by Agrolimen in 2025. Quality is high, but the price premium is difficult to justify against Nom Nom without a compelling ingredient or formulation differentiator. Nom Nom wins on value.

### Spot & Tango
Similar price tier to Nom Nom (~$5.50–7.00/day for a 30-lb dog) with both fresh and air-dried (UnKibble) options. The air-dried line is worth considering for households where freezer logistics are a sticking point. No recalls in 2025 or 2026.

### Purina Pro Plan (Dry Kibble)
The only kibble option in this comparison with feeding-trial AAFCO compliance. Substantial research infrastructure, DACVN involvement, and excellent outcomes at $1.80–2.20/day. If fresh food logistics or cost do not work, Pro Plan is the honest alternative. See our [Best Large Breed Puppy Food 2026: Vet-Tested Growth Formulas](/best-puppy-food-large-breeds-2026-growth-optimized) for growth-specific context.

---

## Verdict and Score: 7.8/10

Nom Nom earns its 7.8 on the strength of a legitimate nutrition team, genuinely calibrated personalization (the spay/neuter adjustment alone puts it ahead of most competitors), documented formula consistency, and real published research backing the gut-health claims. The ingredient quality is high. Bruno's digestion improved measurably, and the weight held stable across 60 days — which is the practical test of portion accuracy.

The score does not reach 8-plus because of three factors that cannot be dismissed: the rubber band packaging issue that persisted unresolved across four deliveries, the freezer and thaw logistics that genuinely limit long-term practicality for many households, and the omega-3 transparency gap that leaves a meaningful nutritional question unanswered for a DACVN-formulated premium product.

If you can accommodate the logistics and the cost, Nom Nom is a well-constructed fresh food option backed by real expertise. If the cost is the primary variable, [The Farmer's Dog](https://petverdict.net/go/farmersdog) closes most of the quality gap at meaningfully lower pricing.

**Score Breakdown:**

| Category | Score |
|----------|-------|
| Ingredient Quality | 8.5/10 |
| Nutritional Formulation | 8.5/10 |
| Portion Personalization | 8.0/10 |
| Palatability | 8.0/10 |
| Packaging & Logistics | 5.5/10 |
| Transparency | 7.0/10 |
| Value for Cost | 6.5/10 |
| **Overall** | **7.8/10** |

---

## FAQ

### Is Nom Nom actually formulated by a veterinary nutritionist?

Yes. Dr. Justin Shmalberg holds the DACVN credential — Diplomat of the American College of Veterinary Nutrition — which is the highest board certification available in pet nutrition. He leads the formulation team alongside PhD nutritionists. This is meaningfully different from brands that claim "vet-developed" based on a single consultant review. The nutritionist involvement at Nom Nom is ongoing, not a one-time credential for marketing purposes.

### Does Nom Nom use feeding trials or just nutrient-profile compliance for AAFCO?

Nom Nom uses the nutrient-profile pathway for AAFCO compliance, not feeding trials. Their statement — "All Nom Nom Freshly-made Food recipes meet or exceed the AAFCO Dog Food Nutrition Profile standards for all life stages" — means the formulated nutrient levels meet AAFCO targets. Feeding trials mean real dogs were fed the food and their health was measured. Purina Pro Plan uses feeding trials; Nom Nom does not. The nutrient-profile pathway is rigorous, and having DACVN oversight adds credibility, but it is an honest distinction to note.

### Who owns Nom Nom, and does that affect quality?

Mars Petcare acquired Nom Nom in 2022 for approximately $1 billion (Bloomberg) and integrated it into the Royal Canin division. Mars Petcare is the largest pet food company in the world by revenue, which brings manufacturing infrastructure and quality control resources that independent fresh food startups cannot match. The formula consistency I observed across four deliveries likely reflects this. Whether the corporate ownership concerns you philosophically is a values question, but the acquisition has not produced a detectable quality decline in the product.

### How does Nom Nom handle the grain-free question?

Nom Nom recipes are not grain-free in the traditional marketing sense — the potato and squash bases are starch sources, but they are not grain. The FDA's ongoing DCM (dilated cardiomyopathy) investigation focused on legume-heavy grain-free diets. Nom Nom's formulas do not rely heavily on peas, lentils, chickpeas, or potatoes as primary protein sources — the concern in the DCM investigation was legume-dense diets where legumes appeared to substitute for animal protein. A DACVN-supervised formula using whole meat protein with vegetable carbohydrate sources is materially different from the diet profile flagged in the FDA investigation. That said, the FDA investigation remains unresolved and inconclusive, and anyone with a cardiac breed should discuss diet composition specifically with their veterinarian.

### Is the first-order discount worth trying Nom Nom?

The Sampler Pack at $20 (as of April 2026) and the 50% off first order are genuinely low-risk entry points. For a 47-lb dog like Bruno, the first order at full discount comes to roughly $100–110 instead of $200–225. This is a reasonable trial investment before committing to the monthly logistics. I would recommend starting with the Sampler Pack if you are unsure about palatability, then moving to the full subscription if your dog accepts it consistently over two weeks.

### How should I manage the transition to Nom Nom from kibble?

I used the 25/75 to 75/25 ratio transition over 14 days, which is conservative but produced the best digestive outcomes in my testing. The more common point of failure is transitioning too quickly — three to five days is not enough for most dogs. Bruno had one loose stool episode around day seven from too-fast a ratio shift; slowing down resolved it within 48 hours. Keep transition day counts conservative, especially if your dog has a history of digestive sensitivity. If your dog eats quickly and tends to bloat or regurgitate, pair fresh food with an Outward Hound Fun Feeder Slow Bowl ([Check price on Amazon](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B00FPKNRCS?tag=petverdict-20)) — the texture of fresh food makes it easy to inhale.

### What do longtime Nom Nom customers say?

The pattern across verified reviews is consistent with what I observed: high satisfaction with ingredient quality and digestion improvements, frustration with packaging logistics and cost at scale. One ConsumerAffairs reviewer described it well: "After 6 months of Nom Nom Dog Food, I am amazed by how healthy, energetic and playful my dogs are." But the same review thread that includes that sentiment also includes the packaging critique: the rubber band damage is a recurring complaint, not an isolated incident. On balance, the food quality earns the positive reviews; the packaging engineering does not.

---

*Prices shown are as of April 2026. Nom Nom pricing varies by dog weight and subscription frequency — verify at nomnomnow.com before ordering. These reviews reflect a 60-day testing window on one primary test dog (Bruno, 47-lb neutered Lab mix) with supplementary palatability data from an extended group. Individual results will vary based on dog age, activity level, health status, and transition protocol. Consult your veterinarian before switching diets, particularly if your dog has a diagnosed health condition or is on prescription medication.*
